 INTELLIGENCE BRIEFING — As of 2025-03-25
 Subject: Donald J. Trump
 Knowledge Window: 2025-01-01 → 2025-03-25
 Events in window: 310 | Showing top 8

[2025-02-10] President Trump Orders Reinstatement of 25% Tariffs on Steel and Steel Derivatives, Increases Aluminum Tariffs to 25%, and Terminates Country Exemptions
President Donald J. Trump signed proclamations on February 10, 2025, reinstating 25% tariffs on all steel imports and increasing tariffs on aluminum imports from 10% to 25%. The actions, taken under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, terminated prior alternative agreements that had provided tariff relief to countries including Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, the European Union, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, Ukraine, and the United Kingdom. These countries will now be subject to the full 25% tariff.

Effective March 12, 2025, all steel, steel derivative products, and aluminum products previously covered by Section 232 will be subject to a 25% tariff. The proclamations also imposed an additional 25% ad valorem duty on certain new derivative steel and aluminum articles. The Commerce Secretary's authority to grant new product exclusion requests or renew existing ones was eliminated, effective immediately. A narrow exclusion was created for derivative steel and aluminum products originating from U.S.-melted or smelted articles. By May 11, 2025, the Commerce Secretary must establish a process for U.S. producers to request the inclusion of additional derivative products under these tariffs.
Key facts:
  • 25 percent tariffs reinstated on all steel imports (Section 232 action)
  • 25% tariff on aluminum imports elevated
  • 25% tariff on steel imports restored
  • 25% tariff reinstated on all steel imports
  • Action based on Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962
  • Additional 25 percent ad valorem duty imposed on certain derivative steel and aluminum articles
  • Affected countries include Australia, South Korea, Canada, Mexico, Brazil, Japan, United Kingdom, European Union
  • Aluminum tariffs increased from 10% to 25%
  • By May 11, 2025, Commerce Secretary must establish a process for U.S. producers to request inclusion of additional derivative products
  • Domestic aluminum capacity utilization fell from 61% in 2019 to 55% in 2023
  • Domestic steel capacity utilization fell to 77.3% in 2022 and 75.3% in 2023
  • Exclusion process for tariffs terminated
  • Exemptions eliminated for Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, European Union, Ukraine, United Kingdom
  • New narrow exclusion for derivative steel/aluminum products originating from U.S.-melted/smelted articles
  • No drawbacks available for duties imposed
  • Original Section 232 tariffs imposed in March 2018 (25% steel, 10% aluminum)
  • Over $10 billion committed to new mills in the U.S. following initial tariffs
  • Presidential proclamation issued on Feb. 10, 2025
  • Prior tariff relief agreements terminated for Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, EU countries, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, Ukraine, and the United Kingdom
  • Removes Commerce Secretary's authority to grant new product exclusions or renew existing ones, effective immediately
  • Revokes country-specific exemptions and quota arrangements from 2018 Section 232 tariffs
  • Secretary of Commerce to establish a process for including additional derivative articles within 90 days
  • Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 (19 U.S.C. § 1862)
  • Steel and aluminum entering Foreign Trade Zones (FTZ) must be entered with 'privileged foreign status'
  • Steel and aluminum imports fell by nearly a third from 2016 to 2020
  • Strict 'melted and poured' standards to be enforced
  • Target capacity utilization of 80% for domestic industries
  • Tariffs apply to goods entered for consumption on or after March 12, 2025
  • Tariffs effective March 12, 2025
  • Tariffs on aluminum products increased from 10 percent to 25 percent (Section 232 action)
  • Two Proclamations signed by President Trump on February 10, 2025
  • United States Customs and Border Protection (CBP) directed to strictly enforce rules and assess maximum penalties for misclassification
Sources: www.bakerdonelson.com, www.hklaw.com, www.whitehouse.gov (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-03-12] Trump's 25% Tariffs on Steel and Aluminum Imports Take Effect, Prompting EU Counter-Measures
U.S. President Donald Trump's 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum imports took effect on Wednesday, March 12, 2025, following a White House confirmation on Tuesday. The duties apply to imports from nations including Canada, Australia, and the European Union.

The European Union announced it would impose counter-tariffs totaling 26 billion euros ($28.33 billion) on U.S. goods starting in April. These EU measures include re-imposing 8 billion euros in previously suspended tariffs and implementing new countermeasures on 18 billion euros of goods.

Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese criticized Trump's tariffs but stated Australia would not impose reciprocal tariffs. The White House also confirmed Trump would not raise tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum imports to 50%, a decision linked to Ontario Premier Doug Ford pausing a 25% tax on electricity exports to the U.S.
Key facts:
  • 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum imports
  • 31 deregulatory actions announced
  • Actions aim to advance President Trump’s Day One executive orders
  • Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese
  • Canadian steel and aluminum tariffs not raised to 50%
  • EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin
  • EU counter-tariffs on 26 billion euros ($28.33 billion) worth of U.S. goods
  • EU new countermeasures on 18 billion euros of goods
  • EU re-imposed tariffs on 8 billion euros of U.S. exports
  • Ending "Good Neighbor Plan"
  • European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen
  • Expected to roll back 'trillions in regulatory costs'
  • Ontario's 25% tax on electricity exports to the U.S.
  • Overhauling Biden-Harris Administration’s “Social Cost of Carbon”
  • Prioritizing coal ash program (CCR Rule)
  • Reconsideration of Biden-Harris Administration Risk Management Program rule
  • Reconsideration of Mercury and Air Toxics Standards (MATS)
  • Reconsideration of Particulate Matter National Ambient Air Quality Standards (PM 2.5 NAAQS)
  • Reconsideration of exceptional events rulemaking
  • Reconsideration of light-duty, medium-duty, and heavy-duty vehicle regulations (Car GHG Rules)
  • Reconsideration of limitations, guidelines and standards (ELG) for the Steam Electric Power Generating Industry
  • Reconsideration of mandatory Greenhouse Gas Reporting Program
  • Reconsideration of multiple National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants (NESHAPs)
  • Reconsideration of regulations on power plants (Clean Power Plan 2.0)
  • Reconsideration of regulations throttling the oil and gas industry (OOOO b/c)
  • Reconsideration of technology transition rule
  • Reconsideration of the 2009 Endangerment Finding
  • Reconsideration of wastewater regulations for oil and gas development (Oil and Gas ELG)
  • Reconstituting Science Advisory Board (SAB) and Clean Air Scientific Advisory Committee (CASAC)
  • Restructuring the Regional Haze Program
  • Terminating Biden’s Environmental Justice and DEI arms of the agency (EJ/DEI)
  • Utilizing enforcement discretion for North Carolina’s recovery from Hurricane Helene
  • White House trade advisor Peter Navarro
  • Working with states and tribes to resolve backlog with State Implementation Plans (SIPs) and Tribal Implementation Plans (TIPs)
"protect European businesses, workers and consumers from the impact of these unjustified trade restrictions" — European Commission (Statement explaining the purpose of EU counter-measures)
"strong but proportionate" — Ursula von der Leyen (Describing the EU's two-pronged approach to counter-tariffs)
"We will always remain open to negotiation" — Ursula von der Leyen (Statement regarding EU counter-measures)
"It's against the spirit of our two nations' enduring friendship and fundamentally at odds with the benefits that our economic partnership has delivered over more than 70 years" — Anthony Albanese (Press conference criticizing Trump's metal tariffs)
"Today is the greatest day of deregulation our nation has seen. We are driving a dagger straight into the heart of the climate change religion to drive down cost of living for American families, unleash American energy, bring auto jobs back to the U.S. and more." — Lee Zeldin (Announcement of 31 deregulatory actions)
"Alongside President Trump, we are living up to our promises to unleash American energy, lower costs for Americans, revitalize the American auto industry, and work hand-in-hand with our state partners to advance our shared mission." — Lee Zeldin (Announcement of 31 deregulatory actions)
Sources: www.cnbc.com, www.epa.gov (2 sources)

[2025-01-20] President Issues Memorandum Declaring OECD Global Tax Deal Has No Force or Effect in US
On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump delivered his second inaugural address, outlining plans for executive orders related to deportations, energy, and tariffs.

On the same day, the President issued a memorandum stating that prior administration commitments regarding the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Global Tax Deal have no force or effect in the United States unless adopted by Congress. The memorandum directed the Treasury Secretary and the U.S. Permanent Representative to the OECD to formally notify the organization of this position. It also instructed the Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the United States Trade Representative, to investigate foreign countries for non-compliance with tax treaties or for implementing extraterritorial tax rules on American companies, requiring a list of responsive actions within 60 days.

Additionally, the administration issued Executive Order 14154, “Unleashing American Energy,” directing federal agencies to immediately pause the disbursement of funds appropriated through the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. This has resulted in slowed funding for various programs and subsequent litigation.
Key facts:
  • 11% of Black voters for Trump (exit polls)
  • 46% of Latino voters for Trump (exit polls)
  • 60-day deadline for recommendations to the President
  • 62% disapproved of Trump pardoning Jan. 6 defendants (NPR poll)

  • Department of Transportation (DOT) suspended new obligations under National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Formula Program on February 6, 2025
  • District Courts for the District of Columbia and Rhode Island enjoined implementation of the OMB memorandum

  • Elon Musk (attendee)
  • Emancipation Hall (location of informal speech)
  • Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) funding paused as of February 7, 2025
  • Executive Order (EO) 14154, "Unleashing American Energy"

[2025-03-04] Trump Administration Implements New Tariffs on Imports from Canada, Mexico, and China, and Global Steel and Aluminum
The Trump administration imposed additional 10% tariffs on all Chinese goods on March 3, 2025, under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. On March 4, the administration implemented new tariffs, increasing duties on Chinese imports from 10% to 20%. Imports from Canada and Mexico faced an additional 25% duty, with Canadian energy resources and critical minerals subject to a 10% tariff. Products qualifying under USMCA from Canada and Mexico were largely excluded. Global steel and aluminum imports will face an extra 25% tariff starting March 12, 2025. Imports qualifying for de minimis entry from Canada, Mexico, and China were temporarily exempt.

China announced retaliatory trade measures on March 4, including new 10-15% tariffs on U.S. agricultural products, effective March 10. China's General Administration of Customs suspended export authorization for three U.S. soybean producers and halted imports of all U.S. logs. China also added 10 U.S. companies to its Unreliable Entities List and 15 U.S. companies to its Control List, imposing various prohibitions and restrictions. Canada and China announced plans for reciprocal tariffs to take effect April 2, 2025, including 25% additional tariffs on lumber and copper.
Key facts:
  • 10 additional US companies added to China's Unreliable Entities List (UEL)
  • 10% tariff on Canadian energy resources and critical minerals
  • 10% tariffs on sorghum, soybeans, pork, beef, seafood, fruit, vegetables, dairy products

[2025-02-03] President Pauses Implementation of Tariffs on Mexican Goods Until March 4
The President on February 3, 2025, issued an order pausing the implementation of additional 25% ad valorem tariffs on products from Mexico. The administration cited Mexico's failure to intercept drug trafficking organizations, human traffickers, and illicit drugs as the reason for the tariffs, which were initially determined on February 1, 2025, under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The President declared the situation an "unusual and extraordinary threat" to U.S. national security, foreign policy, and economy. The pause extends the tariffs' effective date from February 4, 2025, to March 4, 2025, at 12:01 am eastern time. The order cited "immediate steps" taken by the Government of Mexico to address illegal migration and illicit drug crises. The Secretary of Homeland Security will assess the situation during this period, with the order stating tariffs could be immediately implemented if crises worsen or Mexico fails to take sufficient steps.
Key facts:
  • Assessment to be conducted by Secretary of Homeland Security, Secretary of State, Attorney General, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs, and Assistant to the President for Homeland Security
  • Authority cited: International Emergency Economic Powers Act (50 U.S.C. 1701 et seq.) (IEEPA)
  • Authority cited: National Emergencies Act (50 U.S.C. 1601 et seq.)
  • Authority cited: Section 301 of title 3, United States Code
  • Authority cited: Section 604 of the Trade Act of 1974 (19 U.S.C. 2483)

[2025-02-28] Trump and Zelenskyy Clash Over Ukraine War Strategy and US Aid in Contentious Oval Office Meeting
U.S. President Donald Trump, Vice President JD Vance, and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy met in the Oval Office on February 28, 2025. The meeting addressed continued U.S. support for Ukraine and a potential mineral resources agreement. It concluded without a clear resolution. During the discussion, Trump and Vance called for gratitude for U.S. aid and urged diplomacy and an immediate ceasefire. Zelenskyy cited Russia's history of breaking agreements and stated the need for security guarantees. Trump stated Ukraine was not winning the war and required U.S. support for leverage, adding that Russian President Vladimir Putin respected him. Trump told Zelenskyy that Ukraine must reach a deal or face a withdrawal of U.S. support. Following the meeting, the Trump administration suspended intelligence and military aid to Ukraine for approximately one week. Aid resumed after Zelenskyy agreed to an unconditional 30-day ceasefire, which Russia subsequently rejected. U.S. officials, including Secretary of State Marco Rubio, appeared on news shows on March 2, 2025, to support Trump's "America first" approach. European leaders, including European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer, gathered in London on March 2, 2025, to express unified support for Ukraine.
Key facts:
  • Ceasefire signed in 2019 by Zelenskyy, Macron, and Merkel

  • European leaders present at London summit: European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, Finnish President Alexander Stubb, France's President Emmanuel Macron, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, Romania's Interim President Ilie Bolojan, European Council President Antonio Costa, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau

"For four years, the United States of America, we had a president (Joe Biden) who stood up at press conferences and talked tough about Vladimir Putin, and then Putin invaded Ukraine and destroyed a significant chunk of the country." — JD Vance (Opening statement on diplomacy and past US policy)

So during a lot of years — I’m not speaking about just Biden, but those times was Obama, President (Barack) Obama, then President Trump, then President Biden, now President Trump, and God bless, now, President Trump will stop him — but during 2014, nobody stopped him." — Volodymyr Zelenskyy (Responding to Vance's call for diplomacy and historical context of Russian aggression)
"Mr. President, with respect, I think it’s disrespectful for you to come into the Oval Office to try to litigate this in front of the American media. Right now, you guys are going around and forcing conscripts to the front lines because you have manpower problems. You should be thanking the president for trying to bring an end to this conflict." — JD Vance (Criticizing Zelenskyy's demeanor and advocating for gratitude)

"But he didn’t break them with me. He wants to make a deal. I don’t know if you can make a deal. The problem is, I’ve empowered you (turning toward Zelenskyy) to be a tough guy, and I don’t think you’d be a tough guy without the United States. And your people are very brave. But you’re either going to make a deal or we’re out. And if we’re out, you’ll fight it out." — Donald Trump (Discussing Putin's trustworthiness and the conditions of continued US support)

"And if it becomes apparent that President Zelensky’s either personal motivations or political motivations are divergent from ending the fighting in this country, then I think we have a real issue on our hands" — Mike Waltz (Appearance on CNN’s “State of the Union” on March 2, 2025)
"Either he needs to come to his senses and come back to the table in gratitude, or someone else needs to lead the country to do that" — Mike Johnson (Appearance on NBC’s “Meet the Press” on March 2, 2025)
"the shaking of the head, the kind of — the arms crossed, we just — we found it incredibly disrespectful" — Mike Waltz (Sunday statement on March 2, 2025, regarding Zelensky’s actions during the Friday meeting)

I mean … he engaged by saying, you know, people are dying. … We need to stop the dying. … Let’s go forward with the peace negotiations, and then we’ll determine what security arrangements need to be made around the peace negotiations" — Mike Turner (quoting Trump) (Appearance on CBS’ “Face the Nation” on March 2, 2025)

[2025-01-20] Trump Issues America First Trade Policy Memorandum Directing Review of Biden-Era Tech Policies
On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued several directives, including the America First Trade Policy memorandum. The memorandum instructed federal agencies to review technology policies enacted by the Biden administration.

Specific directives included the Commerce Secretary assessing U.S. intellectual property rights for Chinese persons and recommending reciprocal actions, and reviewing connected vehicle rulemaking for expanded controls. The Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the Commerce Secretary, was directed to review President Biden’s outbound investment executive order for potential modification or rescission and to assess existing national security controls. Agencies are required to submit these policy reviews by April 1, 2025.

Concurrently, President Trump issued a memorandum imposing a regulatory freeze. This directive ordered agencies not to propose or issue new rules until reviewed and approved by a Trump-appointed or designated department head. It also mandated the withdrawal of rules sent to the Office of the Federal Register but not yet published, for review and approval. This memorandum was published in the Federal Register on January 28, 2025 (90 FR 8249).

Additionally, President Trump issued a pardon proclamation for certain federal offenses related to the events at the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021. The proclamation allows eligible individuals to apply for a certificate of pardon through the Department of Justice after completing their sentences.
Key facts:

  • Addressed to heads of executive departments and agencies

[2025-03-04] President Trump Delivers 2025 State of the Union Address to Joint Session of Congress
President Donald Trump delivered his 2025 State of the Union address to a joint session of the 119th United States Congress on March 4, 2025. The address, his first of a second non-consecutive term, began at 9:19 p.m. EST in the House chamber of the U.S. Capitol and lasted 1 hour and 39 minutes. Vice President J.D. Vance and Speaker of the House Mike Johnson presided. Trump outlined proposals including border security measures, economic plans such as extending the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and eliminating taxes on tips, and enhanced domestic energy production. He also announced plans to withdraw from international agreements including the Paris Climate Accord and the World Health Organization, and orders making English the official U.S. language and banning men from women's sports. The speech drew an estimated 36.6 million viewers. Secretary of Veterans Affairs Doug Collins was the designated survivor. Democratic Representative Al Green was removed and later censured for disrupting the speech.
Key facts:
  • 119th United States Congress

  • 25% tariffs on imported aluminum, copper, lumber, and steel

  • Claimed DOGE identified $45 million waste for DEI scholarships in Burma
  • Claimed DOGE identified $8 million waste for 'making mice transgender'
  • Claimed actions in 43 days: Nearly 100 Executive Orders, over 400 Executive Actions

"America is back." — Donald Trump (Opening of the 2025 State of the Union address)
"President Trump's message of making America secure and competitive again are welcome words for the people of Michigan." — Representative Tim Walberg (R-MI) (Post-speech reaction to the 2025 State of the Union address)
"We have accomplished more in 43 days than most administrations accomplished in 4 years, 8 years — and we are just getting started." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"Within hours of taking the oath of office, I declared a national emergency on our southern border, and I deployed the U.S. military and Border Patrol to repel the invasion of our country, and what a job they've done." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"And two days ago I signed an order making English the official language of the United States of America." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"I also signed an executive order to ban men from playing in women's sports." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"And to that end I have created the brand new Department of Government Efficiency. DOGE! Perhaps you've heard of it, perhaps. Which is headed by Elon Musk, who is in the gallery tonight." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"Do you want to keep it going for another five years?" — Donald Trump (Questioning continued U.S. funding for Ukraine during his speech)
"Yeah, yeah, you would say, 'Pocahontas says yes.'" — Donald Trump (Referring to Senator Elizabeth Warren during discussion of Ukraine funding)
"I don't know why we can't fully celebrate [...] I think that's something we could all celebrate." — John Fetterman (Fox News interview on March 6, regarding the DJ Daniel incident during the speech)
"quite insulting" — Lejone Mpotjoane (Criticizing Trump's remark referring to Lesotho as a nation 'which nobody has ever heard of')
